PR Description

Fixes #7345.

This PR adds a warning mechanism in the `reproject_to()` method of the `GenericMap` class within SunPy. The enhancement aims to alert users when a `rsun` discrepancy (greater than 200000 m) exists between the input map and the target WCS during the reprojection process. This warning helps prevent potential data loss or unexpected results resulting from `rsun` from sunpy.map import Map
import numpy as np

aiamap = Map("https://git.ias.u-psud.fr/adolliou/report_issue_github/-/raw/main/data/sunpy_reprojection_issue/aia.lev1.171.1497617052.2022-03-17T00_22_03.342Z.image_lev1.fits?ref_type=heads")
hrimap = Map("https://git.ias.u-psud.fr/adolliou/report_issue_github/-/raw/main/data/sunpy_reprojection_issue/solo_L2_eui-hrieuvopn-image_20220317T001800215_V02.fits?ref_type=heads")

aiamap_rsun_ref_val = aiamap.meta["rsun_ref"]
hrimap_rsun_ref_val = hrimap.meta["rsun_ref"]

# Looping through different rsun_ref differences to evaluate has_nan behavior
for i in range(101790, 101800, 1):
    # Modifying aiamap rsun_ref based on the iteration
    aiamap.meta["rsun_ref"] = aiamap_rsun_ref_val - i

    # Calculate the difference in rsun_ref values between the maps
    rsun_diff = abs(aiamap.meta["rsun_ref"] - hrimap_rsun_ref_val)

    # Reprojecting AIA map to HRI map and checking for NaN values
    aia_reprojected = aiamap.reproject_to(hrimap.wcs, algorithm="interpolation")
    has_nan = np.isnan(aia_reprojected.data).any()

    # Print the rsun_diff and has_nan status
    print(f"rsun_diff: {rsun_diff}, has_nan: {has_nan}")
    # aia_reprojected.peek()
```

```python
Output:
rsun_diff: 198210.0, has_nan: True
rsun_diff: 198209.0, has_nan: True
rsun_diff: 198208.0, has_nan: True
rsun_diff: 198207.0, has_nan: True
rsun_diff: 198206.0, has_nan: True
rsun_diff: 198205.0, has_nan: False
rsun_diff: 198204.0, has_nan: False
rsun_diff: 198203.0, has_nan: False
rsun_diff: 198202.0, has_nan: False
rsun_diff: 198201.0, has_nan: False
```

As `rsun_diff` decreased from 198210 to 198201, the `has_nan` transitioned from `True` (indicating data loss) to `False` (no data loss). Notably, at the `rsun_diff` value of 198205, the `has_nan` status changed to `False`, suggesting no data loss beyond this point. Therefore, `rsun_diff` values greater than 198205 consistently caused data loss during reprojection.

Based on the above evaluation I have selected new a threshold of 198205 which I believe is precise compared to 20000. PR Description

Fixes #7345.

This PR adds a warning mechanism in the `reproject_to()` method of the `GenericMap` class within SunPy. The enhancement aims to alert users when a `rsun` discrepancy (greater than 200000 m) exists between the input map and the target WCS during the reprojection process. This warning helps prevent potential data loss or unexpected results resulting from `rsun` PR Description

Fixes #7345.

This PR adds a warning mechanism in the `reproject_to()` method of the `GenericMap` class within SunPy. The enhancement aims to alert users when a `rsun` discrepancy (greater than 200000 m) exists between the input map and the target WCS during the reprojection process. This warning helps prevent potential data loss or unexpected results resulting from `rsun` PR Description

Fixes #7345.

This PR adds a warning mechanism in the `reproject_to()` method of the `GenericMap` class within SunPy. The enhancement aims to alert users when a `rsun` discrepancy (greater than 200000 m) exists between the input map and the target WCS during the reprojection process. This warning helps prevent potential data loss or unexpected results resulting from `rsun` PR Description

Fixes #7345.

This PR adds a warning mechanism in the `reproject_to()` method of the `GenericMap` class within SunPy. The enhancement aims to alert users when a `rsun` discrepancy (greater than 200000 m) exists between the input map and the target WCS during the reprojection process. This warning helps prevent potential data loss or unexpected results resulting from `rsun` PR Description

Fixes #7345.

This PR adds a warning mechanism in the `reproject_to()` method of the `GenericMap` class within SunPy. The enhancement aims to alert users when a `rsun` discrepancy (greater than 200000 m) exists between the input map and the target WCS during the reprojection process. This warning helps prevent potential data loss or unexpected results resulting from `rsun` PR Description

Fixes #7345.

This PR adds a warning mechanism in the `reproject_to()` method of the `GenericMap` class within SunPy. The enhancement aims to alert users when a `rsun` discrepancy (greater than 200000 m) exists between the input map and the target WCS during the reprojection process. This warning helps prevent potential data loss or unexpected results resulting from `rsun` PR Description

Fixes #7345.

This PR adds a warning mechanism in the `reproject_to()` method of the `GenericMap` class within SunPy. The enhancement aims to alert users when a `rsun` discrepancy (greater than 200000 m) exists between the input map and the target WCS during the reprojection process. This warning helps prevent potential data loss or unexpected results resulting from `rsun` PR Description

Fixes #7345.

This PR adds a warning mechanism in the `reproject_to()` method of the `GenericMap` class within SunPy. The enhancement aims to alert users when a `rsun` discrepancy (greater than 200000 m) exists between the input map and the target WCS during the reprojection process. This warning helps prevent potential data loss or unexpected results resulting from `rsun` {"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/astropy/astropy/pulls/comments/1214810050","pull_request_review_id":1458445136,"id":1214810050,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOAB_CCc5IaIfC","diff_hunk":"@@ -0,0 +1,116 @@
+from astropy.cosmology.connect import readwrite_registry
+from astropy.cosmology.core import Cosmology
+from astropy.table import QTable
+from astropy.table.serialize import represent_mixins_as_columns
+
+from .table import from_table, to_table
+
+
+def read_mrt(filename, index=None, *, move_to_meta=False, cosmology=None, **kwargs):
+    """Read a |Cosmology| from an MRT file.
+
+    Parameters
+    ----------
+    filename : path-like or file-like
+        The file from which to read the MRT data.
+    index : int or str or None, optional
+        Needed to select the row in tables with multiple rows. ``index`` can be
+        an integer for the row number or, if the table is indexed by a column,
+        the value of that column. If the table is not indexed and ``index`` is a
+        string, the "name" column is used as the indexing column.
+    move_to_meta : bool, optional keyword-only
+        Whether to move keyword arguments that are not in the Cosmology class'
+        signature to the Cosmology's metadata. This will only be applied if the
+        Cosmology does NOT have a keyword-only argument (e.g. ``**kwargs``).
+        Arguments moved to the metadata will be merged with existing metadata,
+        preferring specified metadata in the case of a merge conflict (e.g. for
+        ``Cosmology(meta={'key':10}, key=42)``, the ``Cosmology.meta`` will be
+        ``{'key': 10}``).
+    cosmology : str or |Cosmology| class or None, optional keyword-only
+        The cosmology class (or string name thereof) to use when constructing
+        the cosmology instance. The class also provides default parameter
+        values, filling in any non-mandatory arguments missing in 'table'.
+    **kwargs : Any
+        Additional keyword arguments passed to :attr:`astropy.table.QTable.read`.
+        The 'format' is set to 'mrt', regardless of input.
+
+    Returns
+    -------
+    |Cosmology| subclass instance
+
+    Raises
+    ------
+    ValueError
+        If the keyword argument 'format' is given and is not "mrt".
+    """
+    # Check that the format is 'mrt' (or not specified)
+    format = kwargs.pop("format", "mrt")
+    if format != "mrt":
+        raise ValueError(f"format must be 'mrt', not {format}")
+
+    # Reading is handled by `QTable`.
+    table = QTable.read(filename, format="mrt", **kwargs)
+
+    # Create a single column named 'm_nu' by combining the values from the 'mnu0', 'mnu1', and 'mnu2' columns
+    m_nu_data = []
+    for i in ("mnu0", "mnu1", "mnu2"):
+        column_data = table[i][0]
+        m_nu_data.append(column_data.value)
+        table.remove_column(i)
+
+    table.add_column([m_nu_data], name="m_nu", index=-2)
+
+    # Build the cosmology from table, using the private backend.
+    return from_table(
+        table, index=index, move_to_meta=move_to_meta, cosmology=cosmology
+    )
+
+
+def write_mrt(cosmology, file, *, overwrite=False, cls=QTable, **kwargs):

+    overwrite : bool, optional keyword-only

+    cls : |Table| class, optional keyword-only

+    **kwargs : Any
+        Additional keyword arguments passed to ``cls.write``.
+
+    Raises

+    TypeError
+        If kwarg (optional) 'cls' is not a subclass of `astropy.table.Table`
+    ValueError
+        If the keyword argument 'format' is given and is not "mrt".
+    """
+    # Check that the format is 'mrt' (or not specified)
+    format = kwargs.pop("format", "mrt")
+    if format != "mrt":
+        raise ValueError(f"format must be 'mrt', not {format}")
+
+    # Set cosmology_in_meta as false for now since there is no metadata being kept
+    table_main = to_table(cosmology, cls=cls, cosmology_in_meta=False)
+    table = represent_mixins_as_columns(table_main)
+
+    # Replace the m_nu column with three columns with names 'mnu0', 'mnu1', 'mnu2'
+    m_nu = table_main["m_nu"]
+    table.remove_column("m_nu")

```

```

This pull request is to address #14871

If this pull request is unrelated to any issues, please remove
the following line. -->

Fixes #14871

Registered the read_mrt() and write_mrt() methods with the Cosmology class, enabling the import and export of cosmological data to and from MRT files.

## Writing Example:
```python
from astropy.cosmology import Planck18
Planck18.write("example_cosmology.mrt", overwrite=True, format="mrt")
```
Output:
```plaintext
Title:
Authors:
Table:
================================================================================
Byte-by-byte Description of file: table.dat
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Bytes Format Units  Label     Explanations
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 1-13  A13          ---    cosmology Description of cosmology
15-22  A8           ---    name      Description of name
24-28  F5.2   km.Mpc-1.s-1 H0        [67.66/67.66] Hubble constant as an
                              `~astropy.units.Quantity` at z=0.
30-36  F7.5         ---    Om0       [0.3/0.31] Omega matter; matter
                              density/critical density at z=0.
38-43  F6.4         K      Tcmb0     [2.72/2.73] Temperature of the CMB as
                              `~astropy.units.Quantity` at z=0.
45-49  F5.3         ---    Neff      [3.04/3.05] Number of effective neutrino
                              species.
51-53  F3.1         eV     mnu0      Description of mnu0
55-57  F3.1         eV     mnu1      Description of mnu1
59-62  F4.2         eV     mnu2      [0.06/0.06] Description of mnu2
64-70  F7.5         ---    Ob0       [0.04/0.05] Omega baryon; baryonic matter
                              density/critical density at z=0.
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Notes:
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
FlatLambdaCDM Planck18 67.66 0.30966 2.7255 3.046 0.0 0.0 0.06 0.04897
```
## Reading Example

```python
from astropy.cosmology import Cosmology
Cosmology.read('example_cosmology.mrt', format='mrt')
```
Output:
```plaintext
FlatLambdaCDM(name="Planck18", H0=67.66 km / (Mpc s), Om0=0.30966, Tcmb0=2.7255 K, Neff=3.046, m_nu=[0.   0.   0.06] eV, Ob0=0.04897)
```

This pull request is to address #14871

<!-- If the pull request closes any open issues you can add this.
If you replace <Issue Number> with a number, GitHub will automatically link it.
If this pull request is unrelated to any issues, please remove
the following line. -->

Fixes #14871

Registered the read_mrt() and write_mrt() methods with the Cosmology class, enabling the import and export of cosmological data to and from MRT files.

## Writing Example:
```python
from astropy.cosmology import Planck18
Planck18.write("example_cosmology.mrt", overwrite=True, format="mrt")
```
Output:
```plaintext
Title:
Authors:
Table:
================================================================================
Byte-by-byte Description of file: table.dat
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Bytes Format Units  Label     Explanations
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 1-13  A13          ---    cosmology Description of cosmology
15-22  A8           ---    name      Description of name
24-28  F5.2   km.Mpc-1.s-1 H0        [67.66/67.66] Hubble constant as an
                              `~astropy.units.Quantity` at z=0.
30-36  F7.5         ---    Om0       [0.3/0.31] Omega matter; matter
                              density/critical density at z=0.
38-43  F6.4         K      Tcmb0     [2.72/2.73] Temperature of the CMB as
                              `~astropy.units.Quantity` at z=0.
45-49  F5.3         ---    Neff      [3.04/3.05] Number of effective neutrino
                              species.
51-53  F3.1         eV     mnu0      Description of mnu0
55-57  F3.1         eV     mnu1      Description of mnu1
59-62  F4.2         eV     mnu2      [0.06/0.06] Description of mnu2
64-70  F7.5         ---    Ob0       [0.04/0.05] Omega baryon; baryonic matter
                              density/critical density at z=0.
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Notes:
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
FlatLambdaCDM Planck18 67.66 0.30966 2.7255 3.046 0.0 0.0 0.06 0.04897
```
## Reading Example

```python
from astropy.cosmology import Cosmology
Cosmology.read('example_cosmology.mrt', format='mrt')
```
Output:
```plaintext
FlatLambdaCDM(name="Planck18", H0=67.66 km / (Mpc s), Om0=0.30966, Tcmb0=2.7255 K, Neff=3.046, m_nu=[0.   0.   0.06] eV, Ob0=0.04897)
```

This pull request is to address #14871

<!-- If the pull request closes any open issues you can add this.
If you replace <Issue Number> with a number, GitHub will automatically link it.
If this pull request is unrelated to any issues, please remove
the following line. -->

Fixes #14871

Registered the read_mrt() and write_mrt() methods with the Cosmology class, enabling the import and export of cosmological data to and from MRT files.

## Writing Example:
```python
from astropy.cosmology import Planck18
Planck18.write("example_cosmology.mrt", overwrite=True, format="mrt")
```
Output:
```plaintext
Title:
Authors:
Table:
================================================================================
Byte-by-byte Description of file: table.dat
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Bytes Format Units  Label     Explanations
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 1-13  A13          ---    cosmology Description of cosmology
15-22  A8           ---    name      Description of name
24-28  F5.2   km.Mpc-1.s-1 H0        [67.66/67.66] Hubble constant as an
                              `~astropy.units.Quantity` at z=0.
30-36  F7.5         ---    Om0       [0.3/0.31] Omega matter; matter
                              density/critical density at z=0.
38-43  F6.4         K      Tcmb0     [2.72/2.73] Temperature of the CMB as
                              `~astropy.units.Quantity` at z=0.
45-49  F5.3         ---    Neff      [3.04/3.05] Number of effective neutrino
                              species.
51-53  F3.1         eV     mnu0      Description of mnu0
55-57  F3.1         eV     mnu1      Description of mnu1
59-62  F4.2         eV     mnu2      [0.06/0.06] Description of mnu2
64-70  F7.5         ---    Ob0       [0.04/0.05] Omega baryon; baryonic matter
                              density/critical density at z=0.
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Notes:
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
FlatLambdaCDM Planck18 67.66 0.30966 2.7255 3.046 0.0 0.0 0.06 0.04897
```
## Reading Example

```python
from astropy.cosmology import Cosmology
Cosmology.read('example_cosmology.mrt', format='mrt')
```
Output:
```plaintext
FlatLambdaCDM(name="Planck18", H0=67.66 km / (Mpc s), Om0=0.30966, Tcmb0=2.7255 K, Neff=3.046, m_nu=[0.   0.   0.06] eV, Ob0=0.04897)
```

This pull request is to address #14871

<!-- If the pull request closes any open issues you can add this.

If this pull request is unrelated to any issues, please remove
the following line. -->

Fixes #14871

Registered the read_mrt() and write_mrt() methods with the Cosmology class, enabling the import and export of cosmological data to and from MRT files.

## Writing Example:
```python
from astropy.cosmology import Planck18
Planck18.write("example_cosmology.mrt", overwrite=True, format="mrt")
```
Output:
```plaintext
Title:
Authors:
Table:
================================================================================
Byte-by-byte Description of file: table.dat
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Bytes Format Units  Label     Explanations
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 1-13  A13          ---    cosmology Description of cosmology
15-22  A8           ---    name      Description of name
24-28  F5.2   km.Mpc-1.s-1 H0        [67.66/67.66] Hubble constant as an
                              `~astropy.units.Quantity` at z=0.
30-36  F7.5         ---    Om0       [0.3/0.31] Omega matter; matter
                              density/critical density at z=0.
38-43  F6.4         K      Tcmb0     [2.72/2.73] Temperature of the CMB as
                              `~astropy.units.Quantity` at z=0.
45-49  F5.3         ---    Neff      [3.04/3.05] Number of effective neutrino
                              species.
51-53  F3.1         eV     mnu0      Description of mnu0
55-57  F3.1         eV     mnu1      Description of mnu1
59-62  F4.2         eV     mnu2      [0.06/0.06] Description of mnu2
64-70  F7.5         ---    Ob0       [0.04/0.05] Omega baryon; baryonic matter
                              density/critical density at z=0.
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Notes:
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
FlatLambdaCDM Planck18 67.66 0.30966 2.7255 3.046 0.0 0.0 0.06 0.04897
```
## Reading Example

```python
from astropy.cosmology import Cosmology
Cosmology.read('example_cosmology.mrt', format='mrt')
```
Output:
```plaintext
FlatLambdaCDM(name="Planck18", H0=67.66 km / (Mpc s), Om0=0.30966, Tcmb0=2.7255 K, Neff=3.046, m_nu=[0.   0.   0.06] eV, Ob0=0.04897)
```

This pull request is to address #14871

<!-- If the pull request closes any open issues you can add this.
If you replace <Issue Number> with a number, GitHub will automatically link it.
If this pull request is unrelated to any issues, please remove
the following line. -->

Fixes #14871

Registered the read_mrt() and write_mrt() methods with the Cosmology class, enabling the import and export of cosmological data to and from MRT files.

## Writing Example:
```python
from astropy.cosmology import Planck18
Planck18.write("example_cosmology.mrt", overwrite=True, format="mrt")
```
Output:
```plaintext
Title:
Authors:
Table:
================================================================================
Byte-by-byte Description of file: table.dat
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Bytes Format Units  Label     Explanations
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 1-13  A13          ---    cosmology Description of cosmology
15-22  A8           ---    name      Description of name
24-28  F5.2   km.Mpc-1.s-1 H0        [67.66/67.66] Hubble constant as an
                              `~astropy.units.Quantity` at z=0.
30-36  F7.5         ---    Om0       [0.3/0.31] Omega matter; matter
                              density/critical density at z=0.
38-43  F6.4         K      Tcmb0     [2.72/2.73] Temperature of the CMB as
                              `~astropy.units.Quantity` at z=0.
45-49  F5.3         ---    Neff      [3.04/3.05] Number of effective neutrino
                              species.
51-53  F3.1         eV     mnu0      Description of mnu0
55-57  F3.1         eV     mnu1      Description of mnu1
59-62  F4.2         eV     mnu2      [0.06/0.06] Description of mnu2
64-70  F7.5         ---    Ob0       [0.04/0.05] Omega baryon; baryonic matter
                              density/critical density at z=0.
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Notes:
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
FlatLambdaCDM Planck18 67.66 0.30966 2.7255 3.046 0.0 0.0 0.06 0.04897
```
## Reading Example

```python
from astropy.cosmology import Cosmology
Cosmology.read('example_cosmology.mrt', format='mrt')
```
Output:
```plaintext
FlatLambdaCDM(name="Planck18", H0=67.66 km / (Mpc s), Om0=0.30966, Tcmb0=2.7255 K, Neff=3.046, m_nu=[0.   0.   0.06] eV, Ob0=0.04897)
```

Fixes #12355 

I've added a new write_latex() method to the Cosmology package that allows you to export a Cosmology object to a Latex table. 

Test:
```python
from astropy.cosmology import Planck18
from astropy.cosmology.io import latex 

latex.write_latex(Planck18, "dd.tex", overwrite=True, latex_names=True)
```
Output:
```latex
\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{cccccccc}
cosmology & name & $$H_0 
{[\mathrm{km\,s^{-1}\,Mpc^{-1}}]}$$ & Om0 & $$T_{0} 
{[\mathrm{K}]}$$ & Neff & $$m_{nu} 
{[\mathrm{eV}]}$$ & Ob0 \\
FlatLambdaCDM & Planck18 & 67.66 & 0.30966 & 2.7255 & 3.046 & 0.0 .. 0.06 & 0.04897 \\
\end{tabular}
\end{table}
```

Before I move on to tests I think I should add the read_latex() and latex_identify() methods to the Cosmology package as well. Please let me know if it is necessary or if I should jump on to tests. I came up with the code below by referencing the read_html_table().

```python
def read_latex(filename, index=None, *, move_to_meta=False, cosmology=None,**kwargs):
    # Check that the format is 'ascii.html' (or not specified)
    format = kwargs.pop("format", "latex")
    if format != "latex":
        raise ValueError(f"format must be 'latex', not {format}")
    
    with u.add_enabled_units(cu):
        table = QTable.read(filename, format="latex", **kwargs)

    table_columns = set(table.colnames)
    for name, latex in _FORMAT_TABLE.items():
        if latex in table_columns:
            table.rename_column(latex, name)
            
    # Build the cosmology from table, using the private backend.
    return from_table(table, index=index, move_to_meta=move_to_meta, cosmology=cosmology)
```

Fixes #12355 

I've added a new write_latex() method to the Cosmology package that allows you to export a Cosmology object to a Latex table. 

Test:
```python
from astropy.cosmology import Planck18
from astropy.cosmology.io import latex 

latex.write_latex(Planck18, "dd.tex", overwrite=True, latex_names=True)
```
Output:
```latex
\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{cccccccc}
cosmology & name & $$H_0 
{[\mathrm{km\,s^{-1}\,Mpc^{-1}}]}$$ & Om0 & $$T_{0} 
{[\mathrm{K}]}$$ & Neff & $$m_{nu} 
{[\mathrm{eV}]}$$ & Ob0 \\
FlatLambdaCDM & Planck18 & 67.66 & 0.30966 & 2.7255 & 3.046 & 0.0 .. 0.06 & 0.04897 \\
\end{tabular}
\end{table}
```

Please let me know if you have any suggestions.
```python
# Licensed under a 3-clause BSD style license - see LICENSE.rst

# THIRD PARTY
import pytest

# LOCAL
import astropy.units as u
from astropy.cosmology.io.latex import _FORMAT_TABLE, write_latex
from astropy.table import QTable
from astropy.cosmology import Planck15

class TestWriteLatex:
    def setup_class(self):
        # Create a cosmology object to use for testing
        self.cosmo = Planck15

    def test_write_latex(self, tmp_path):
        # Write the cosmology to a temporary file
        filename = tmp_path / "test_write_latex.tex"
        write_latex(self.cosmo, filename)

        # Read the table back in using QTable.read() and check that the data is correct
        table = QTable.read(filename, format="latex")
        
        for column_name in table.colnames[2:]:  
            assert column_name in _FORMAT_TABLE.values()

    def test_write_latex_bad_cls(self, tmp_path):
        # Test that passing an invalid class to write_latex() raises a TypeError
        filename = tmp_path / "test_write_latex_bad_cls.tex"
        with pytest.raises(TypeError):
            write_latex(self.cosmo, filename, cls=str)

    def test_write_latex_invalid_path(self):
        # Test that passing an invalid path to write_latex() raises a IOError
        invalid_path = ""

        with pytest.raises(IOError):
            write_latex(self.cosmo, invalid_path, overwrite=True)

    def test_write_latex_invalid_format(self, tmp_path):
        filename = tmp_path / "test_write_latex_invalid_format.tex"
        # Test using non-Latex format
        with pytest.raises(ValueError):
            write_latex(self.cosmo, filename, format="ascii.html")
```

This pull request is to address Issue #12355 

<!-- If the pull request closes any open issues you can add this.
If you replace <Issue Number> with a number, GitHub will automatically link it.
If this pull request is unrelated to any issues, please remove
the following line. -->

Fixes #12355 

I've added a new write_latex() method to the Cosmology package that allows you to export a Cosmology object to a Latex table. 

Test:
```python
from astropy.cosmology import Planck18
from astropy.cosmology.io import latex 

latex.write_latex(Planck18, "dd.tex", overwrite=True, latex_names=True)
```
Output:
```latex
\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{cccccccc}
cosmology & name & $$H_0 
{[\mathrm{km\,s^{-1}\,Mpc^{-1}}]}$$ & Om0 & $$T_{0} 
{[\mathrm{K}]}$$ & Neff & $$m_{nu} 
{[\mathrm{eV}]}$$ & Ob0 \\
FlatLambdaCDM & Planck18 & 67.66 & 0.30966 & 2.7255 & 3.046 & 0.0 .. 0.06 & 0.04897 \\
\end{tabular}
\end{table}
```

This pull request is to address Issue #12355 

<!-- If the pull request closes any open issues you can add this.
If you replace <Issue Number> with a number, GitHub will automatically link it.
If this pull request is unrelated to any issues, please remove
the following line. -->

Fixes #12355 

I've added a new write_latex() method to the Cosmology package that allows you to export a Cosmology object to a Latex table. 

Test:
```python
from astropy.cosmology import Planck18
from astropy.cosmology.io import latex 

latex.write_latex(Planck18, "dd.tex", overwrite=True, latex_names=True)
```
Output:
```latex
\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{cccccccc}
cosmology & name & $$H_0 
{[\mathrm{km\,s^{-1}\,Mpc^{-1}}]}$$ & Om0 & $$T_{0} 
{[\mathrm{K}]}$$ & Neff & $$m_{nu} 
{[\mathrm{eV}]}$$ & Ob0 \\
FlatLambdaCDM & Planck18 & 67.66 & 0.30966 & 2.7255 & 3.046 & 0.0 .. 0.06 & 0.04897 \\
\end{tabular}
\end{table}
```

def read_latex(filename, index=None, *, move_to_meta=False, cosmology=None,**kwargs):
    format = kwargs.pop("format", "latex")
    if format != "latex":
        raise ValueError(f"format must be 'latex', not {format}")
    
    with u.add_enabled_units(cu):
        table = QTable.read(filename, format="latex", **kwargs)

    table_columns = set(table.colnames)
    for name, latex in _FORMAT_TABLE.items():
        if latex in table_columns:
            table.rename_column(latex, name)

```

What is the problem this feature will solve?

[Ruff](https://beta.ruff.rs/docs/) is a Python linter that helps to improve and maintain code quality by checking that the code complies with a customizable subset of its [lint rules](https://beta.ruff.rs/docs/rules/). `astropy` has chosen to apply all Ruff rules unless they are explicitly ignored. Some of the rules `astropy` has chosen to not apply at all, but the ones listed in [.ruff.toml](https://github.com/astropy/astropy/blob/main/.ruff.toml) are currently being ignored simply because no-one has fixed the violations in the `astropy` codebase.

### Describe the desired outcome

[.ruff.toml](https://github.com/astropy/astropy/blob/main/.ruff.toml) in `astropy` is meant to be a temporary file. Everything in that file should eventually be either addressed in the code base or permanently moved to [pyproject.toml](https://github.com/astropy/astropy/blob/main/pyproject.toml) (see #14736). At the moment many of the ignored rules are violated in only a handful of places in the code base, so they can be addressed without too much effort.

### Additional context

To see all the Ruff rule violations in `astropy` run
```shell
ruff --select=ALL --statistics .
```
This will produce a list of rules that `astropy` currently does not comply with ordered by the number of violations. The rule you choose to address must then be deleted from the ignore list in [.ruff.toml](https://github.com/astropy/astropy/blob/main/.ruff.toml) and violations must be fixed in the code base. Ruff can fix some of the violations automatically, e.g.
```shell
ruff --select=C417 --fix .
```
Note that Ruff is in active development and some of the automatic fixes might not have been available at the time the rule was added to the ignore list. For the rule violations that cannot be automatically fixed their location in the code base can be revealed, e.g.
```shell
ruff --select=PT015 --show-source .
```

If a rule has only a handful of violations then it can be fixed together with other similar rules. For example, in the current `astropy` `main` (commit cc73b24619ce37f2af26a0140bbdda8015ac8265) the rules PTH108, PTH109 and PTH114 have two violations each and they can be fixed together in the same pull request. On the other hand some of the rules have a large number of violations, e.g. 257 for PTH123. In such cases it is preferable to address the rules one sub-package per pull request. In the example commands above `.` should then be replaced with the sub-package name, e.g.
```shell
ruff --select=PTH118 --show-source astropy/table
```

If a rule is not in the ignore list in [.ruff.toml](https://github.com/astropy/astropy/blob/main/.ruff.toml) and is instead in the ignore list in [pyproject.toml](https://github.com/astropy/astropy/blob/main/pyproject.toml) then it should not be addressed.

### Suggestions for first-time contributors

The following list does not claim to be complete and might be expanded in the future.
- [ ] C400
- [ ] C406
- [ ] C408
- [ ] C413
- [ ] C416
- [ ] C414
- [ ] C417
- [ ] ISC001
- [ ] ISC003
- [ ] PIE790
- [ ] PIE804
- [ ] PIE810
- [ ] PLW0120
- [ ] RET504
- [ ] RET505
- [ ] RET506

Use `ruff rule` to find out more about the listed rules. For example,
```shell
$ ruff rule ISC001
# single-line-implicit-string-concatenation (ISC001)

Derived from the **flake8-implicit-str-concat** linter.

## What it does
Checks for implicitly concatenated strings on a single line.

## Why is this bad?
While it is valid Python syntax to concatenate multiple string or byte
literals implicitly (via whitespace delimiters), it is unnecessary and
negatively affects code readability.

In some cases, the implicit concatenation may also be unintentional, as
autoformatters are capable of introducing single-line implicit
concatenations when collapsing long lines.

## Example
z = "The quick " "brown fox."

Use instead:
z = "The quick brown fox."

## PR summary
This pull request is to address Issue #25836

The current behavior of the Animation Examples Gallery in the documentation is causing an inconsistent navigation experience. When clicking on any of the animation examples, the side navigation section displays links to the matplotlib API reference instead of the examples gallery. This PR removes the API reference links in the animation_api.rst which should fix Issue#25836

Refactored the code in the astropy/io sub-package to address the Ruff rule C408 violation. Replaced unnecessary dict calls with corresponding literals.
This pull request is to address Issue #14840

<!-- If the pull request closes any open issues you can add this.
If you replace <Issue Number> with a number, GitHub will automatically link it.

you @codingtosh for reviewing my PR. I believe that the problem lies with the examples mentioned in the [animation_api.rst](https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/blob/1539614d4c81dad47970e34a76eb5c377146ea7a/doc/api/animation_api.rst?plain=1#L15-L16) file. They seem to be causing the website to break, while the examples not mentioned there work just fine. Here is a list of examples referenced in the animation_api.rst: - Decay - The Bayes update - The double pendulum problem - Animated histogram - Rain simulation - Animated 3D random walk - Animated line plot - Oscilloscope - MATPLOTLIB UNCHAINED - Animated image using a precomputed list of images - Frame grabbing And here is the list of examples not referenced in animation_api.rst - pyplot animation - Multiple axes animation - pasuing and resuming an animation - animated scatter saved as gif Based on this observation, it appears that the examples mentioned in animation_api.rst are the root cause of the problem. The solution that I am proposing involves removing the examples from the animation_api.rst file entirely. By doing so, we can effectively break the linkage between the Example links and API links, which should resolve the issue with the side navigation. Additionally, this solution has the advantage of providing cleaner side navigation for the API references, as shown in the following example: https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/48038715/239146952-a8db6b1d-605a-4bbc-9ae0-f52ef564da45.png If there's a specific need to reference Example links in animation_api.rst, we can brainstorm some alternative approaches to achieve that while keeping the navigation section intact. I'm open to discussion. Fixes #14840","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/astropy/astropy/issues/14841/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/astropy/astropy/issues/14841/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/astropy/astropy/issues/comments/1559059395","html_url":"https://github.com/astropy/astropy/pull/14841#issuecomment-1559059395","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/astropy/astropy/issues/14841","id":1559059395,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAB_CCc5c7VvD","user":{"login":"Mubin17","id":48038715,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ4MDM4NzE1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/48038715?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Mubin17","html_url":"https://github.com/Mubin17","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Mubin17/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Mubin17/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Mubin17/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Mubin17/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Mubin17/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Mubin17/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Mubin17/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Mubin17/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Mubin17/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-05-23T10:58:05Z","updated_at":"2023-05-23T10:58:05Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"> I notice this pull request has two commits: the first fixes the rule violations in `astropy.io`, the second edits `.ruff.toml`. [If `pre-commit` is set up](https://docs.astropy.org/en/latest/development/workflow/development_workflow.html#pre-commit) then it would be simpler to first edit `.ruff.toml` and then to make the commit without manually editing any other files. If Ruff can autofix the rule (it can autofix C408) then the Ruff `pre-commit` hook automatically fixes the rule violations and the Black hook automatically fixes any code style issues (if any are needed).

Thanks @eerovaher for the suggestion! I feel like editing the .ruff.toml file first and then committing the changes without manual file editing sounds like a much smoother workflow. I'll definitely keep this in mind when I tackle Ruff rule-C408 in other Astropy sub-packages.

Just a quick question: Do you think the current pull request is ready to be merged, or if there are any additional changes or considerations you would like me to address?

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt def rainbow_text(x, y, strings, colors, orientation='horizontal', ax=None, **kwargs): """ Take a list of *strings* and *colors* and place them next to each other, with text strings[i] being shown in colors[i]. Parameters ---------- x, y : float Text position in data coordinates. strings : list of str The strings to draw. colors : list of color The colors to use. orientation : {'horizontal', 'vertical'} ax : Axes, optional The Axes to draw into. If None, the current axes will be used. **kwargs All other keyword arguments are passed to plt.annotate(), so you can set the font size, family, etc. """ if ax is None: ax = plt.gca() assert orientation in ['horizontal', 'vertical'] if orientation == 'vertical': kwargs.update(rotation=90, verticalalignment='bottom') for s, c in zip(strings, colors): ax.annotate(s + " ", (x, y), color=c, **kwargs) if orientation == 'horizontal': x += 0.1 else: y += 0.1 words = "all unicorns poop rainbows ! ! !".split() colors = ['red', 'orange', 'gold', 'lawngreen', 'lightseagreen', 'royalblue', 'blueviolet'] plt.figure(figsize=(6, 6), dpi=100) rainbow_text(0.1, 0.05, words, colors, size=18) rainbow_text(0.05, 0.1, words, colors, orientation='vertical', size=18) plt.show() ``` @anntzer The code above is my attempt to rewrite rainbow_text simpler fashion using annotate(), however, I am having an issue formatting text appropriately within the figure. Do have any suggestions on how can I fix that? Thanks","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/comments/1563874487/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

The I followed your steps and the results overall look good to me. 

```python
from astropy.cosmology import Planck18
from astropy.io import ascii

# Step 1: Convert cosmology object to a table
table = Planck18.to_format("astropy.table")

# Step 2: Replace the m_nu column with N columns
m_nu = table['m_nu']  # Get the m_nu column
N = len(m_nu[0])  # Get the number of elements in m_nu

# Remove the m_nu column from the table
table.remove_column('m_nu')

# Add N new columns to the table with names 'm_nu_0', 'm_nu_1', 'm_nu_2', ...
for i in range(N):
    column_name = f'm_nu_{i}'
    column_data = m_nu[0][i]
    table.add_column(column_data, name=column_name, index=-2)
    
print(table)
# Step 3: Save as an MRT
ascii.write(table, "ascii_write_mrt_check.mrt", format="mrt", overwrite=True)
```
The output for print(table) looks like this:
```plaintext
  name        H0        Om0   Tcmb0   Neff m_nu_0 m_nu_1 m_nu_2   Ob0  
         km / (Mpc s)           K            eV     eV     eV          
-------- ------------ ------- ------ ----- ------ ------ ------ -------
Planck18        67.66 0.30966 2.7255 3.046    0.0    0.0   0.06 0.04897
```
However, the ascii_write_mrt_check.mrt file has Unknown labels in place of actual labels like H0, Tcmb0, etc. Is this because of units?

> Also, I haven't read the spec carefully. What's meant to be in the notes section; can we put the metadata there?

Yes, we can but I believe ascii.write() method doesn’t support passing the metadata directly as a keyword argument. As Mrt documentation says ["Note that the metadata of the table, apart from units, column names and description, will not be written. These have to be filled in by hand later."](https://docs.astropy.org/en/stable/io/ascii/write.html)

I ran into an issue while using ascii.write from astropy.io.ascii to save a table in the Machine Readable Table (MRT) format. The problem arises when attempting to write the column labels, as they are not correctly identified in the resulting MRT file. Instead, the labels are displayed as "Unknown."

### Expected behavior

The resulting MRT file should have the column labels correctly displayed, corresponding to the column names in the table.

### How to Reproduce

Run the code below:
It will convert the Planck18 cosmology object into a table, replace the m_nu column with multiple columns, and save the resulting table in the MRT format as a file named ascii_write_mrt_check.mrt.
```python
from astropy.cosmology import Planck18
from astropy.io import ascii

# Step 1: Convert cosmology object to a table
table = Planck18.to_format("astropy.table")

# Step 2: Replace the m_nu column with N columns
m_nu = table['m_nu']  # Get the m_nu column
N = len(m_nu[0])  # Get the number of elements in m_nu

# Remove the m_nu column from the table
table.remove_column('m_nu')

# Add N new columns to the table with names 'm_nu_0', 'm_nu_1', 'm_nu_2', ...
for i in range(N):
    column_name = f'm_nu{i}'
    column_data = m_nu[0][i]
    table.add_column(column_data, name=column_name, index=-2)
    
# Step 3: Save as an MRT
ascii.write(table, "ascii_write_mrt_check.mrt", format="mrt", overwrite=True)
```
Check ascii_write_mrt_check.mrt file.

```plaintext
Title:
Authors:
Table:
================================================================================
Byte-by-byte Description of file: table.dat
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Bytes Format Units  Label     Explanations
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 1- 8  A8           ---    name    Description of name
10-14  F5.2   km.Mpc-1.s-1 Unknown [67.66/67.66] Hubble constant as an
                            `~astropy.units.Quantity` at z=0.
16-22  F7.5         ---    Om0     [0.3/0.31] Omega matter; matter
                            density/critical density at z=0.
24-29  F6.4         K      Unknown [2.72/2.73] Temperature of the CMB as
                            `~astropy.units.Quantity` at z=0.
31-35  F5.3         ---    Neff    [3.04/3.05] Number of effective neutrino
                            species.
37-39  F3.1         eV     Unknown Description of Unknown
41-43  F3.1         eV     Unknown Description of Unknown
45-48  F4.2         eV     Unknown [0.06/0.06] Description of Unknown
50-56  F7.5         ---    Ob0     [0.04/0.05] Omega baryon; baryonic matter
                            density/critical density at z=0.
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Notes:
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Planck18 67.66 0.30966 2.7255 3.046 0.0 0.0 0.06 0.04897
```

### Versions

Numpy 1.24.3
pyerfa 2.0.0.3

This pull request is to address #14871

<!-- If the pull request closes any open issues you can add this.
If you replace <Issue Number> with a number, GitHub will automatically link it.
If this pull request is unrelated to any issues, please remove
the following line. -->

Fixes #14871

Registered the read_mrt() and write_mrt() methods with the Cosmology class, enabling the import and export of cosmological data to and from MRT files.

## Writing Example:
```python
from astropy.cosmology import Planck18
Planck18.write("example_cosmology.mrt", overwrite=True, format="mrt")
```
Output:
```plaintext
Title:
Authors:
Table:
================================================================================
Byte-by-byte Description of file: table.dat
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Bytes Format Units  Label     Explanations
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 1-13  A13          ---    cosmology Description of cosmology
15-22  A8           ---    name      Description of name
24-28  F5.2   km.Mpc-1.s-1 H0        [67.66/67.66] Hubble constant as an
                              `~astropy.units.Quantity` at z=0.
30-36  F7.5         ---    Om0       [0.3/0.31] Omega matter; matter
                              density/critical density at z=0.
38-43  F6.4         K      Tcmb0     [2.72/2.73] Temperature of the CMB as
                              `~astropy.units.Quantity` at z=0.
45-49  F5.3         ---    Neff      [3.04/3.05] Number of effective neutrino
                              species.
51-53  F3.1         eV     mnu0      Description of mnu0
55-57  F3.1         eV     mnu1      Description of mnu1
59-62  F4.2         eV     mnu2      [0.06/0.06] Description of mnu2
64-70  F7.5         ---    Ob0       [0.04/0.05] Omega baryon; baryonic matter
                              density/critical density at z=0.
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Notes:
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
FlatLambdaCDM Planck18 67.66 0.30966 2.7255 3.046 0.0 0.0 0.06 0.04897
```
## Reading Example

```python
from astropy.cosmology import Cosmology
Cosmology.read('example_cosmology.mrt', format='mrt')
```
Output:
```plaintext
FlatLambdaCDM(name="Planck18", H0=67.66 km / (Mpc s), Om0=0.30966, Tcmb0=2.7255 K, Neff=3.046, m_nu=[0.   0.   0.06] eV, Ob0=0.04897)
```

Fixes #14536

EarthLocation.get_itrs() function has been updated to allow for the creation of topocentric ITRS coordinates, enabling easier calculations of AltAz coordinates for Earth-based sources.

Fixes #12355 

I've added a new write_latex() method to the Cosmology package that allows you to export a Cosmology object to a Latex table. 

Test:
```python
from astropy.cosmology import Planck18
from astropy.cosmology.io import latex 

latex.write_latex(Planck18, "dd.tex", overwrite=True, latex_names=True)
```
Output:
\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{cccccccc}
cosmology & name & $$H_0 
{[\mathrm{km\,s^{-1}\,Mpc^{-1}}]}$$ & Om0 & $$T_{0} 
{[\mathrm{K}]}$$ & Neff & $$m_{nu} 
{[\mathrm{eV}]}$$ & Ob0 \\
FlatLambdaCDM & Planck18 & 67.66 & 0.30966 & 2.7255 & 3.046 & 0.0 .. 0.06 & 0.04897 \\
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

Please let me know if you have any feedback or suggestions for improvements.

This pull request is to address Issue #12355 

<!-- If the pull request closes any open issues you can add this.
If you replace <Issue Number> with a number, GitHub will automatically link it.
If this pull request is unrelated to any issues, please remove
the following line. -->

Fixes #12355 

I've added a new write_latex() method to the Cosmology package that allows you to export a Cosmology object to a Latex table. 

Test:
```python
from astropy.cosmology import Planck18
from astropy.cosmology.io import latex 

latex.write_latex(Planck18, "dd.tex", overwrite=True, latex_names=True)
```
Output:
```latex
\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{cccccccc}
cosmology & name & $$H_0 
{[\mathrm{km\,s^{-1}\,Mpc^{-1}}]}$$ & Om0 & $$T_{0} 
{[\mathrm{K}]}$$ & Neff & $$m_{nu} 
{[\mathrm{eV}]}$$ & Ob0 \\
FlatLambdaCDM & Planck18 & 67.66 & 0.30966 & 2.7255 & 3.046 & 0.0 .. 0.06 & 0.04897 \\
\end{tabular}
\end{table}
```

This pull request is to address Issue #12355 

<!-- If the pull request closes any open issues you can add this.
If you replace <Issue Number> with a number, GitHub will automatically link it.
If this pull request is unrelated to any issues, please remove
the following line. -->

Fixes #12355 

I've added a new write_latex() method to the Cosmology package that allows you to export a Cosmology object to a Latex table. 

Test:
```python
from astropy.cosmology import Planck18
from astropy.cosmology.io import latex 

latex.write_latex(Planck18, "dd.tex", overwrite=True, latex_names=True)
```
Output:
```latex
\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{cccccccc}
cosmology & name & $$H_0 
{[\mathrm{km\,s^{-1}\,Mpc^{-1}}]}$$ & Om0 & $$T_{0} 
{[\mathrm{K}]}$$ & Neff & $$m_{nu} 
{[\mathrm{eV}]}$$ & Ob0 \\
FlatLambdaCDM & Planck18 & 67.66 & 0.30966 & 2.7255 & 3.046 & 0.0 .. 0.06 & 0.04897 \\
\end{tabular}
\end{table}
```
